    The space_left_action parameter tells the system what action to take when the

    system has detected that it is starting to get low on disk space. Valid values are ignore,

    syslog, rotate, email, exec, suspend, single, and halt.

    •

    ignore, the audit daemon does nothing

    •

    syslog, the audit daemon will issue a warning to syslog

    •

    rotate, the audit daemon will rotate logs, losing the oldest to free up space

    •

    email, the audit daemon will send a warning to the email account specified in

    action_mail_acct as well as sending the message to syslog

    •

    exec, /path-to-script will execute the script. You cannot pass parameters to the

    script. The script is also responsible for telling the auditd daemon to resume

    logging once its completed its action

    •

    suspend, the audit daemon will stop writing records to the disk

    •

    single, the audit daemon will put the computer system in single user mode

    •

    halt, the audit daemon will shut down the system

    The admin_space_left_action parameter tells the system what action to take when

    the system has detected that it is low on disk space. Valid values are ignore, syslog,

    rotate, email, exec, suspend, single, and halt.

    •

    ignore, the audit daemon does nothing

    •

    syslog, the audit daemon will issue a warning to syslog

    •

    rotate, the audit daemon will rotate logs, losing the oldest to free up space

    •

    email, the audit daemon will send a warning to the email account specified in

    action_mail_acct as well as sending the message to syslog

    •

    exec, /path-to-script will execute the script. You cannot pass parameters to the

    script. The script is also responsible for telling the auditd daemon to resume

    logging once its completed its action

    •

    suspend, the audit daemon will stop writing records to the disk

    •

    single, the audit daemon will put the computer system in single user mode

    •

    halt, the audit daemon will shut down the system

    理由

    In high security contexts, the risk of detecting unauthorized access or nonrepudiation

    exceeds the benefit of the system's availability.

    影响

    If the admin_space_left_action is set to single the audit daemon will put the

    computer system in single user mode.

    审计

    Run the following command and verify the space_left_action is set to email, exec,

    single, or halt:

    # grep -P -- '^\h*space_left_action\h*=\h*(email|exec|single|halt)\b'

    /etc/audit/auditd.conf

    Verify the output is email, exec, single, or halt

    Example output

    space_left_action = email

    Run the following command and verify the admin_space_left_action is set to single

  • OR - halt:

    • # grep -P -- '^\h*admin_space_left_action\h*=\h*(single|halt)\b'

    /etc/audit/auditd.conf

    Verify the output is single or halt

    Example output:

    admin_space_left_action = single

    Note: A Mail Transfer Agent (MTA) must be installed and configured properly to set

    space_left_action = email

    修复措施

    Set the space_left_action parameter in /etc/audit/auditd.conf to email, exec,

    single, or halt:

    Example:

    space_left_action = email

    Set the admin_space_left_action parameter in /etc/audit/auditd.conf to

    single or halt:

    Example:

    admin_space_left_action = single

    Note: A Mail Transfer Agent (MTA) must be installed and configured properly to set

    space_left_action = email

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-2, AU-8, AU-12, SI-5

    2. AUDITD.CONF(5)

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.2 Collect Audit Logs

    Collect audit logs. Ensure that logging, per the enterprise’s audit log

    management process, has been enabled across enterprise assets.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    v8

    8.3 Ensure Adequate Audit Log Storage

    Ensure that logging destinations maintain adequate storage to comply with

    the enterprise’s audit log management process.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3 配置 auditd 规则

    原文: Configure auditd Rules

    The Audit system operates on a set of rules that define what is to be captured in the log

    files. The following types of Audit rules can be specified:

    •

    Control rules: Allow the Audit system's behavior and some of its configuration to

    be modified.

    •

    File system rules: Allow the auditing of access to a particular file or a directory

    (Also known as file watches).

    The syntax of these watch rules generally follow this format: -a always,exit -

    F arch=b64 -F path=path-to-file -F perm=permissions -F

    key=keyname

    •

    System call rules: Allow logging of system calls that any specified program

    makes.

    Syscall rules take the general form of: -a action,list -S syscall -F

    field=value -k keyname

    Audit rules can be set:

    •

    On the command line using the auditctl utility, these rules are not persistent

    across reboots.

    •

    In a file ending in .rules in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory.

    Note:

    •

    The rule must start with -a always,exit

    •

    The arch= field e.g. arch=b64 is optional but strongly recommended for best

    performance. b64 is used in the example, but the arch can also be what is on

    your system (x86_64, aarch64, ppc64le, s390x, etc).

    •

    The field -S all will be added by auditctl if it is not included in the rule.

    •

    The rule must include the path= field shown in the example output.

    •

    The rule must include the perm= field shown in the example output. The options

    e.g. wa may include additional options e.g. rwxa

    •

    The key= field e.g. key=scope is optional but strongly recommended. Though the

    name scope is used in the example, this can be set to any name.

    •

    The -w option e.g. -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k scope is deprecated. While the

    deprecated -w is in a "passing" state, it is recommended to update the rule in

    accordance with the Remediation Procedure as the -w will soon be unsupported.

    The following script creates and runs a function called f_audit_rules_format_chk

    that may be used to determine if audit rules have deprecated -w format or missing

    arch= option: 

#!/usr/bin/env bash

    f_audit_rules_format_chk()

    { 
   rules_dir="/etc/audit/rules.d" 
   deprecated_flag="-w" 
   a_output=() 
   for file in "$rules_dir"/*rules; do

    [[ -e "$file" ]] || continue

    while IFS= read -r line; do  
   # Skip empty or commented lines

    [[ "$line" =~ ^\s*# ]] || [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue

    # Check for deprecated -w

    if [[ "$line" == *"$deprecated_flag"* ]]; then

    a_output+=("Deprecated '-w' found in $file

    Rule:

    $(wrap_text "$line")

    Flag: $deprecated_flag")

    fi

    # Check for missing -F arch

    if [[ "$line" =~ ^-a[[:space:]]+always,exit ]] && [[ "$line" != *"-F 
arch="* ]] \

    ; then

    a_output+=("Missing '-F arch=' in $file

    Rule:

    $(wrap_text "$line")

    Flag: Missing arch")

    fi 
   done < "$file" 
   done  
   if [ ${#a_output[@]} -eq 0 ]; then

    echo -e "No issues found in audit rules files"

    else

    echo -e "\nAudit rules issue detected:"

    for output in "${a_output[@]}"; do

    echo -e "\n$output"

    done 
   fi  
}

    # wrap_text function for formatting

    wrap_text() {

    echo "$1"

    }

    f_audit_rules_format_chk

    6.2.3.1 确保 modification of the /etc/sudoers file is collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure modification of the /etc/sudoers file is collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor scope changes for system administrators. If the system has been properly

    configured to force system administrators to log in as themselves first and then use the

    sudo command to execute privileged commands, it is possible to monitor changes in

    scope. The file /etc/sudoers, or files in /etc/sudoers.d, will be written to when the

    file(s) or related attributes have changed. The audit records will be tagged with the

    identifier "scope".

    理由

    Changes in the /etc/sudoers and /etc/sudoers.d files can indicate that an

    unauthorized change has been made to the scope of system administrator activity.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000004-GPOS-00004, SRG-OS-

    000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031,

    SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-

    GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000470-GPOS-00214, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-

    OS-000239-GPOS-00089, SRG-OS-000240-GPOS-00090, SRG-OS-000241-GPOS-

    00091, SRG-OS-000303-GPOS-00120, SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, CCI-002884,

    SRG-OS-000466-GPOS-00210, SRG-OS-000476-GPOS-00221

    审计

    Note: The -w option e.g. -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k scope is deprecated and the

    rule should be updated in accordance with the Remediation Procedure

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/sudoers'

    Verify output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/sudoers -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F dir=/etc/sudoers.d -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/sudoers' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-scope.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/sudoers -F perm=wa -k scope 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-scope.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F 
dir=/etc/sudoers.d -F perm=wa -k scope

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor scope changes for system

    administrators.

    Example:

    # printf '%s\n' "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/sudoers -F perm=wa -k scope

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/sudoers.d -F perm=wa -k scope

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-scope.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030171 | RULE ID: SV-230409r627750 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030172 | RULE ID: SV-230410r627750 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654215 | RULE ID: SV-258217r1045436 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654220 | RULE ID: SV-258218r1045439 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-004970 | RULE ID: SV-269129r1050011 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: ALMA-09-006070 | RULE ID: SV-269135r1050017 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654220 | RULE ID: SV-260646r991575 | CAT II

    10. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654225 | RULE ID: SV-260647r991575 | CAT II

    11. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900510 | RULE ID: SV-270807r1066910 | CAT II

    12. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900520 | RULE ID: SV-270808r1067100 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.8 Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    原文: Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    Membership

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert when an account is added to

    or removed from any group assigned administrative privileges.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.2 确保 actions as another user are always logged 自动化

    原文: Ensure actions as another user are always logged (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor login and logout events. The parameters below track changes to files

    associated with login/logout events.

    •

    /var/log/lastlog - maintain records of the last time a user successfully logged

    in.

    •

    /var/run/faillock - directory maintains records of login failures via the

    pam_faillock module.

    理由

    Monitoring login/logout events could provide a system administrator with information

    associated with brute force attacks against user logins.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000473-

    GPOS-00218

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '(lastlog|faillock)'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/log/lastlog -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/run/faillock -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '(lastlog|faillock)' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-login.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/var/log/lastlog -F perm=wa -k logins 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-login.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=bs4 -S all -F 
path=/var/run/faillock -F perm=wa -k logins

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor login and logout events.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/log/lastlog -F perm=wa -k

    logins

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/run/faillock -F perm=wa -k

    logins

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-login.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030600 | RULE ID: SV-230467r627750 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654255 | RULE ID: SV-258225r1014990 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-047540 | RULE ID: SV-269470r1050353 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900260 | RULE ID: SV-270797r1066880 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654215 | RULE ID: SV-260645r958446 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    影响

    Both the audit and remediation section of this recommendation will traverse all mounted

    file systems that is not mounted with either noexec or nosuid mount options. If there

    are large file systems without these mount options, such traversal will be significantly

    detrimental to the performance of the system.

    Before running either the audit or remediation section, inspect the output of the following

    command to determine exactly which file systems will be traversed:

    # findmnt -n -l -k -it $(awk '/nodev/ { print $2 }' /proc/filesystems | paste

    -sd,) | grep -Pv "noexec|nosuid"

    To exclude a particular file system due to adverse performance impacts, update the

    audit and remediation sections by adding a sufficiently unique string to the grep

    statement. The above command can be used to test the modified exclusions.

    4.9 Log and Alert on Unsuccessful Administrative Account

    原文: Log and Alert on Unsuccessful Administrative Account

    6.2.3.3 确保 events that modify the sudo log file are collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure events that modify the sudo log file are collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor the sudo log file. If the system has been properly configured to disable the use

    of the su command and force all administrators to have to log in first and then use sudo

    to execute privileged commands, then all administrator commands will be logged to

    /var/log/sudo.log . Any time a command is executed, an audit event will be

    triggered as the /var/log/sudo.log file will be opened for write and the executed

    administration command will be written to the log.

    理由

    Changes in /var/log/sudo.log indicate that an administrator has executed a

    command or the log file itself has been tampered with. Administrators will want to

    correlate the events written to the audit trail with the records written to

    /var/log/sudo.log to verify if unauthorized commands have been executed.

    审计

    Note:

    •

    This recommendation requires that the sudo logfile is configured. For additional

    information see guidance provided in the recommendation "Ensure sudo log file

    exists"

    1. Run the following command to verify that sudo has a custom log file configured:

    # grep -rPsi

    "^\h*Defaults\h+([^#]+,\h*)?logfile\h*=\h*(\"|\')?\H+(\"|\')?(,\h*\H+\h*)*\h*

    (#.*)?$" /etc/sudoers*

    Example output:

    Defaults logfile="/var/log/sudo.log"

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'sudo.log'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/log/sudo.log -F perm=wa

    3. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'sudo.log' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-sudo.rules: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/var/log/sudo.log -F perm=wa -k sudo_log_file

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    This recommendation requires that the sudo logfile is configured. For additional

    information see guidance provided in the recommendation "Ensure sudo log file

    exists".

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit the file /etc/sudoers or a file in /etc/sudoers.d/ with visudo -f <PATH

    TO FILE> and add the following line:

    Defaults logfile="<PATH TO CUSTOM LOG FILE>"

    Example:

    Defaults logfile="/var/log/sudo.log"

    2. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify the sudo log file.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/log/sudo.log -F perm=wa -k

    sudo_log_file

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-sudo.rules

    Note: path= should be set to the same sudo log file location as configured above in

    step 1.

    3. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    4. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654235 | RULE ID: SV-260649r986298 | CAT II

    2. STIG ID: UBTU-24-500010 | RULE ID: SV-270740r1066709 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.9 Log and Alert on Unsuccessful Administrative Account

    Login

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert on unsuccessful logins to an

    administrative account.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.4 确保 events that modify date and time information are

    原文: Ensure events that modify date and time information are

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Capture events where the system date and/or time has been modified. The parameters

    in this section are set to determine if the;

    •

    adjtimex - tune kernel clock

    •

    settimeofday - set time using timeval and timezone structures

    •

    stime - using seconds since 1/1/1970

    •

    clock_settime - allows for the setting of several internal clocks and timers

    system calls have been executed. Further, ensure to write an audit record to the

    configured audit log file upon exit, tagging the records with a unique identifier such as

    "time-change".

    理由

    Unexpected changes in system date and/or time could be a sign of malicious activity on

    the system.

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    Run the following command to verify a rule exits in the running configuration:

    1a. Verify a rule exists for adjtimex, settimeofday, clock_settime in the running

    configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '(adjtimex|settimeofday|clock_settime)'

    Verify output of matches:

    -a always,exit -S adjtimex,settimeofday

    -a always,exit -S clock_settime -F a0=0x0

    1b. verify a rule for time-change exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/localtime'

    Verify the output matches:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/localtime -F perm=wa

    Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    2a. Run the following command to verify a rule exists for adjtimex, settimeofday,

    clock_settime in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '(adjtimex|settimeofday|clock_settime)'

    /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-time-change.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    adjtimex,settimeofday -k time-change

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-time-change.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    clock_settime -F a0=0x0 -k time-change

    2b. Run the following command to verify a rule exists for /etc/localtime in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/localtime' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-time-change.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/localtime -F perm=wa -k localtime-change

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules extension,

    with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify date and time information.

    1a. Create a rule for adjtimex, settimeofday, clock_settime:

    Example

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S adjtimex,settimeofday -k time-change

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S clock_settime -F a0=0x0 -k time-change

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-time-change.rules

    1b. Create a rule for /etc/localtime:

    Example

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/localtime -F perm=wa -k

    localtime-change

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-local-time-change.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, CM-6

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement 自动化 Configuration Monitoring

    原文: Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.5 确保 events that modify sethostname and

    原文: Ensure events that modify sethostname and

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record changes to network environment files or system calls. The below parameters

    monitors the following system calls, and write an audit event on system call exit:

    •

    sethostname - set the systems host name

    •

    setdomainname - set the systems domain name

    理由

    Monitoring sethostname and setdomainname will identify potential unauthorized

    changes to host and domain name of a system. The changing of these names could

    potentially break security parameters that are set based on those names.

    审计

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'sethostname|setdomainname'

    Verify the output matches:

    -a always,exit -S sethostname,setdomainname

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'sethostname|setdomainname' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    sethostname,setdomainname -k system-locale

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify the system's

    network environment.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S sethostname,setdomainname -k system-locale

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-system_locale.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, CM-6

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.6 确保 events that modify /etc/issue and /etc/issue.net are

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/issue and /etc/issue.net are

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record changes to network environment files or system calls. The files being monitored

    are:

    •

    /etc/issue and /etc/issue.net - messages displayed pre-login

    理由

    Monitoring /etc/issue and /etc/issue.net is important, as intruders could put

    disinformation into those files and trick users into providing information to the intruder.

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/issue'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/issue -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/issue.net -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/issue' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_issue_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F

    arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/issue -F perm=wa -k system-locale 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_issue_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F 
arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/issue.net -F perm=wa -k system-locale

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify the system's

    network environment.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/issue -F perm=wa -k system-

    locale

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/issue.net -F perm=wa -k

    system-locale

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_issue_system_locale.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, CM-6

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.7 确保 events that modify /etc/hosts and /etc/hostname

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/hosts and /etc/hostname

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record changes to network environment files or system calls. The files being monitored

    are:

    •

    /etc/hosts - file containing host names and associated IP addresses

    •

    /etc/hostname - file contains the system's host name

    理由

    The /etc/hosts file is monitored for changes that can indicate an unauthorized intruder

    is trying to change machine associations with IP addresses and trick users and

    processes into connecting to unintended machines.

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/host'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/hosts -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/hostname -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/host' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_host_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64

    -S all -F path=/etc/hosts -F perm=wa -k system-locale

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_host_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64

    -S all -F path=/etc/hostname -F perm=wa -k system-locale

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify the system's

    network environment.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/hosts -F perm=wa -k system-

    locale

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/hostname -F perm=wa -k system-

    locale

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_host_system_locale.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, CM-6

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.8 确保 events that modify the system's network

    原文: Ensure events that modify the system's network

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record changes to network environment files or system calls. The files being monitored

    are:

    •

    /etc/network/interfaces - This file describes the network interfaces available

    on your

    system # and how to activate them.

    •

    /etc/network/interfaces.d/ - directory containing network interface scripts

    and configurations files

    •

    /etc/netplan/ - central location for YAML networking configurations files

    理由

    Monitoring the systems network environment is important as it can show if network

    interfaces or scripts are being modified in a way that can lead to the machine becoming

    unavailable or compromised. All audit records should have a relevant tag associated

    with them.

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '\/etc/net'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/network/interfaces -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F dir=/etc/network/interfaces.d -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F dir=/etc/netplan/ -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '\/etc/net' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_sysconfig_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F

    arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/network/interfaces -F perm=wa -k system-locale 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_sysconfig_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F 
arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/network/interfaces.d -F perm=wa -k system-locale 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_sysconfig_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -F 
arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/netplan/ -F perm=wa -k system-locale

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify the system's

    network environment.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/network/interfaces -F perm=wa

    -k system-locale

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/network/interfaces.d -F perm=wa

    -k system-locale

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/netplan/ -F perm=wa -k system-

    locale

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_sysconfig_system_locale.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, CM-6

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.9 确保 events that modify /etc/NetworkManager directory

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/NetworkManager directory

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record changes to network environment files or system calls. The files being monitored

    are:

    •

    /etc/NetworkManager/ - directory contains configuration files and settings used

    by the NetworkManager

    理由

    Monitoring the /etc/NetworkManager/ directory will identify potential unauthorized

    changes to network configuration files.

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '\/etc/NetworkManager'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F dir=/etc/NetworkManager/ -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '\/etc\/NetworkManager' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_NetworkManager_system_locale.rules:-a always,exit -

    F arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/NetworkManager/ -F perm=wa -k system-locale

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify the system's

    network environment.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/NetworkManager/ -F perm=wa -k

    system-locale

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-etc_NetworkManager_system_locale.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, CM-6

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.10 确保 use of privileged commands are collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure use of privileged commands are collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor privileged programs, those that have the setuid and/or setgid bit set on

    execution, to determine if unprivileged users are running these commands.

    理由

    Execution of privileged commands by non-privileged users could be an indication of

    someone trying to gain unauthorized access to the system.

    影响

    Both the audit and remediation section of this recommendation will traverse all mounted

    file systems that is not mounted with either noexec or nosuid mount options. If there

    are large file systems without these mount options, such traversal will be significantly

    detrimental to the performance of the system.

    Before running either the audit or remediation section, inspect the output of the following

    command to determine exactly which file systems will be traversed:

    # findmnt -n -l -k -it $(awk '/nodev/ { print $2 }' /proc/filesystems | paste

    -sd,) | grep -Pv "noexec|nosuid"

    To exclude a particular file system due to adverse performance impacts, update the

    audit and remediation sections by adding a sufficiently unique string to the grep

    statement. The above command can be used to test the modified exclusions.

    审计

    On disk configuration

    Run the following script to check on disk rules:

    #!/usr/bin/env bash 
 
{ 
   for PARTITION in $(findmnt -n -l -k -it $(awk '/nodev/ { print $2 }'

    /proc/filesystems | paste -sd,) | grep -Pv "noexec|nosuid" | awk '{print

    $1}'); do

    for PRIVILEGED in $(find "${PARTITION}" -xdev -perm /6000 -type f); do 
         grep -qr "${PRIVILEGED}" /etc/audit/rules.d && printf "OK:

    '${PRIVILEGED}' found in auditing rules.\n" || printf "Warning:

    '${PRIVILEGED}' not found in on disk configuration.\n"

    done 
   done 
}

    Verify that all output is OK.

    Running configuration

    Run the following script to check loaded rules:

    #!/usr/bin/env bash 
 
{

    RUNNING=$(auditctl -l)

    [ -n "${RUNNING}" ] && for PARTITION in $(findmnt -n -l -k -it $(awk

    '/nodev/ { print $2 }' /proc/filesystems | paste -sd,) | grep -Pv

    "noexec|nosuid" | awk '{print $1}'); do

    for PRIVILEGED in $(find "${PARTITION}" -xdev -perm /6000 -type f); do 
         printf -- "${RUNNING}" | grep -q "${PRIVILEGED}" && printf "OK:

    '${PRIVILEGED}' found in auditing rules.\n" || printf "Warning:

    '${PRIVILEGED}' not found in running configuration.\n"

    done 
   done \ 
   || printf "ERROR: Variable 'RUNNING' is unset.\n" 
}

    Verify that all output is OK.

    Special mount points

    If there are any special mount points that are not visible by default from findmnt as per

    the above audit, said file systems would have to be manually audited.

    修复措施

    Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules extension,

    with the relevant rules to monitor the use of privileged commands.

    Example script:

    #!/usr/bin/env bash 
 
{

    UID_MIN=$(awk '/^\s*UID_MIN/{print $2}' /etc/login.defs)

    AUDIT_RULE_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/50-privileged.rules"

    NEW_DATA=()

    for PARTITION in $(findmnt -n -l -k -it $(awk '/nodev/ { print $2 }'

    /proc/filesystems | paste -sd,) | grep -Pv "noexec|nosuid" | awk '{print

    $1}'); do

    readarray -t DATA < <(find "${PARTITION}" -xdev -perm /6000 -type f | awk

    -v UID_MIN=${UID_MIN} '{print "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=" $1

    " -F perm=x -F auid>="UID_MIN" -F auid!=unset -k privileged" }')

    for ENTRY in "${DATA[@]}"; do

    NEW_DATA+=("${ENTRY}")

    done 
  done

    readarray &> /dev/null -t OLD_DATA < "${AUDIT_RULE_FILE}"

    COMBINED_DATA=( "${OLD_DATA[@]}" "${NEW_DATA[@]}" )

    printf '%s\n' "${COMBINED_DATA[@]}" | sort -u > "${AUDIT_RULE_FILE}" 
}

    Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    Check if reboot is required.

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    Special mount points

    If there are any special mount points that are not visible by default from just scanning /,

    change the PARTITION variable to the appropriate partition and re-run the remediation.

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, AU-3(1)

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.11 确保 unsuccessful file access attempts are collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure unsuccessful file access attempts are collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor for unsuccessful attempts to access files. The following parameters are

    associated with system calls that control files:

    •

    creation - creat

    •

    opening - open , openat

    •

    truncation - truncate , ftruncate

    An audit log record will only be written if all of the following criteria is met for the user

    when trying to access a file:

    •

    a non-privileged user (auid>=``UID_MIN)

    •

    is not a Daemon event (auid=``4294967295/unset/-1)

    •

    if the system call returned EACCES (permission denied) or EPERM (some other

    permanent error associated with the specific system call)

    理由

    Failed attempts to open, create or truncate files could be an indication that an individual

    or process is trying to gain unauthorized access to the system.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206,

    SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000064-GPOS-00033

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify UID_MIN is set in /etc/login.defs

    # awk '/^\s*UID_MIN/{print $2}' /etc/login.defs

    Example output:

    1000

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '(EACCES|EPERM)'

    Verify the output matches:

    -a always,exit -S creat,open,openat,truncate,ftruncate -F exit=-EACCES -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    -a always,exit -S creat,open,openat,truncate,ftruncate -F exit=-EPERM -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    3. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '(EACCES|EPERM)' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-access.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    creat,open,openat,truncate,ftruncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset -k access

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-access.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    creat,open,openat,truncate,ftruncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset -k access

    修复措施

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Edit the /etc/login.defs file and create or add the UID_MIN setting.

    Example

    UID_MIN 1000

    2. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor unsuccessful file access attempts.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S creat,open,openat,truncate,ftruncate -F exit=-

    EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S creat,open,openat,truncate,ftruncate -F exit=-

    EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-access.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    3. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    4. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030420 | RULE ID: SV-230449r810455 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654070 | RULE ID: SV-258188r1045349 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048090 | RULE ID: SV-269475r1050358 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654165 | RULE ID: SV-260635r958446 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900160 | RULE ID: SV-270787r1068378 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    14.9 Enforce Detail Logging for Access or Changes to

    Sensitive Data

    Enforce detailed audit logging for access to sensitive data or changes to

    sensitive data (utilizing tools such as File Integrity Monitoring or Security

    Information and Event Monitoring).

    ●

    6.2.3.12 确保 events that modify /etc/group information are

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/group information are

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record events affecting the modification of user or group information, including that of

    passwords and old passwords if in use.

    •

    /etc/group - system groups

    理由

    Unexpected changes to these files could be an indication that the system has been

    compromised and that an unauthorized user is attempting to hide their activities or

    compromise additional accounts.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000004-GPOS-00004, SRG-OS-

    000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031,

    SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-

    GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000470-GPOS-00214, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-

    OS-000239-GPOS-00089, SRG-OS-000240-GPOS-00090, SRG-OS-000241-GPOS-

    00091, SRG-OS-000303-GPOS-00120, SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-

    000476-GPOS-00221

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/group'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/group -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/group' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/group -F perm=wa -k identity

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify user/group

    information.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/group -F perm=wa -k identity

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/manpages/nsswitch.conf.5.en.html

    2. https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-

    us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/managing_smart_cards/pam_configuration_fil

    es

    3. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    4. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030170 | RULE ID: SV-230408r1017214 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654225 | RULE ID: SV-258227r1014992 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-005080 | RULE ID: SV-269129r1050011 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654130 | RULE ID: SV-260628r958368 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-24-200290 | RULE ID: SV-270685r1066544 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.8 Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    Membership

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert when an account is added to

    or removed from any group assigned administrative privileges.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.13 确保 events that modify /etc/passwd information are

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/passwd information are

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record events affecting the modification of user or group information, including that of

    passwords and old passwords if in use.

    •

    /etc/passwd - system users

    理由

    Unexpected changes to these files could be an indication that the system has been

    compromised and that an unauthorized user is attempting to hide their activities or

    compromise additional accounts.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000004-GPOS-00004, SRG-OS-

    000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031,

    SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-

    GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000470-GPOS-00214, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-

    OS-000239-GPOS-00089, SRG-OS-000240-GPOS-00090, SRG-OS-000241-GPOS-

    00091, SRG-OS-000303-GPOS-00120, SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-

    000476-GPOS-00221

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/passwd'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/passwd -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/passwd' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/passwd -F perm=wa -k identity

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify user/group

    information.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/passwd -F perm=wa -k identity

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/manpages/nsswitch.conf.5.en.html

    2. https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-

    us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/managing_smart_cards/pam_configuration_fil

    es

    3. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    4. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030150 | RULE ID: SV-230406r1017212 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654240 | RULE ID: SV-258230r958408 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-005410 | RULE ID: SV-269133r1050015 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654145 | RULE ID:SV-260631r958368 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-24-200280| RULE ID: SV-270684r1066541 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.8 Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    Membership

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert when an account is added to

    or removed from any group assigned administrative privileges.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.14 确保 events that modify /etc/shadow and /etc/gshadow

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/shadow and /etc/gshadow

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record events affecting the modification of user or group information, including that of

    passwords and old passwords if in use.

    •

    /etc/gshadow - encrypted password for each group

    •

    /etc/shadow - system user passwords

    理由

    Unexpected changes to these files could be an indication that the system has been

    compromised and that an unauthorized user is attempting to hide their activities or

    compromise additional accounts.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000004-GPOS-00004, SRG-OS-

    000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031,

    SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-

    GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000470-GPOS-00214, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-

    OS-000239-GPOS-00089, SRG-OS-000240-GPOS-00090, SRG-OS-000241-GPOS-

    00091, SRG-OS-000303-GPOS-00120, SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-

    000476-GPOS-00221

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*shadow'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/gshadow -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/shadow -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'shadow' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/gshadow -F perm=wa -k identity 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F 
path=/etc/shadow -F perm=wa -k identity

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify user/group

    information.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/gshadow -F perm=wa -k identity

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/shadow -F perm=wa -k identity

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/manpages/nsswitch.conf.5.en.html

    2. https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-

    us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/managing_smart_cards/pam_configuration_fil

    es

    3. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    4. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030130 | RULE ID: SV-230404r1017210 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654245 | RULE ID: SV-258231r1069375 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-005960 | RULE ID: SV-269134r1050016 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654150 | RULE ID: SV-260632r958368 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-24-200300 | RULE ID: SV-270686r1066547 | CAT II

    10. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030160 | RULE ID: SV-230407r1017213 | CAT II

    11. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654230 | RULE ID: SV-258228r991572 | CAT II

    12. STIG ID: ALMA-09-005190 | RULE ID: SV-269131r1050013 | CAT II

    13. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654135 | RULE ID: SV-260629r958368 | CAT II

    14. STIG ID: UBTU-24-200310 | RULE ID: SV-270687r1066550 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.8 Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    Membership

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert when an account is added to

    or removed from any group assigned administrative privileges.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.15 确保 events that modify /etc/security/opasswd are

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/security/opasswd are

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record events affecting the modification of user or group information, including that of

    passwords and old passwords if in use.

    •

    /etc/security/opasswd - storage of old passwords if the relevant PAM module

    is in use

    理由

    Unexpected changes to these files could be an indication that the system has been

    compromised and that an unauthorized user is attempting to hide their activities or

    compromise additional accounts.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000004-GPOS-00004, SRG-OS-

    000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031,

    SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-

    GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000470-GPOS-00214, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-

    OS-000239-GPOS-00089, SRG-OS-000240-GPOS-00090, SRG-OS-000241-GPOS-

    00091, SRG-OS-000303-GPOS-00120, SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-

    000476-GPOS-00221

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/security\/opasswd'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/security/opasswd -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/security\/opasswd' 
/etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/security/opasswd -F perm=wa -k identity

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify user/group

    information.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/security/opasswd -F perm=wa -k

    identity

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/manpages/nsswitch.conf.5.en.html

    2. https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-

    us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/managing_smart_cards/pam_configuration_fil

    es

    3. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    4. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030140| RULE ID: SV-230405r1017211 | CAT II

    6. STIIG ID: RHEL-09-654235 | RULE ID: SV-258229r958434 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-005300 | RULE ID: SV-269132r1050014 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654140 | RULE ID: SV-260630r958368 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-24-200320 | RULE ID: SV-270688r1066553 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项
