    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.8 Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    Membership

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert when an account is added to

    or removed from any group assigned administrative privileges.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.16 确保 events that modify /etc/nsswitch.conf file are

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/nsswitch.conf file are

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record events affecting the modification of user or group information, including that of

    passwords and old passwords if in use.

    •

    /etc/nsswitch.conf - file configures how the system uses various databases

    and name resolution mechanisms

    理由

    Unexpected changes to these files could be an indication that the system has been

    compromised and that an unauthorized user is attempting to hide their activities or

    compromise additional accounts.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000004-GPOS-00004, SRG-OS-

    000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031,

    SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-

    GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000470-GPOS-00214, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-

    OS-000239-GPOS-00089, SRG-OS-000240-GPOS-00090, SRG-OS-000241-GPOS-

    00091, SRG-OS-000303-GPOS-00120, SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-

    000476-GPOS-00221

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/nsswitch.conf'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/nsswitch.conf -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'nsswitch.conf' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/nsswitch.conf -F perm=wa -k identity

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify user/group

    information.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/nsswitch.conf -F perm=wa -k

    identity

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/manpages/nsswitch.conf.5.en.html

    2. https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-

    us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/managing_smart_cards/pam_configuration_fil

    es

    3. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    4. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.8 Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    Membership

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert when an account is added to

    or removed from any group assigned administrative privileges.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.17 确保 events that modify /etc/pam.conf and /etc/pam.d/

    原文: Ensure events that modify /etc/pam.conf and /etc/pam.d/

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Record events affecting the modification of user or group information, including that of

    passwords and old passwords if in use.

    •

    /etc/pam.conf - file determines the authentication services to be used, and the

    order in which the services are used.

    •

    /etc/pam.d - directory contains the PAM configuration files for each PAM-aware

    application.

    理由

    Unexpected changes to these files could be an indication that the system has been

    compromised and that an unauthorized user is attempting to hide their activities or

    compromise additional accounts.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000004-GPOS-00004, SRG-OS-

    000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031,

    SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-

    GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000470-GPOS-00214, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-

    OS-000239-GPOS-00089, SRG-OS-000240-GPOS-00090, SRG-OS-000241-GPOS-

    00091, SRG-OS-000303-GPOS-00120, SRG-OS-000304-GPOS-00121, SRG-OS-

    000476-GPOS-00221

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/pam'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/pam.conf -F perm-wa

    -a always,exit -F dir=/etc/pam.d -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/etc\/pam' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/pam.conf -F perm-wa -k identity 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F 
dir=/etc/pam.d -F perm=wa -k identity

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify user/group

    information.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/pam.conf -F perm=wa -k

    identity

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/pam.d -F perm=wa -k identity

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-identity.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/manpages/nsswitch.conf.5.en.html

    2. https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-

    us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/managing_smart_cards/pam_configuration_fil

    es

    3. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    4. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.8 Log and Alert on Changes to Administrative Group

    Membership

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert when an account is added to

    or removed from any group assigned administrative privileges.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.18 确保 discretionary access control permission

    原文: Ensure discretionary access control permission

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor changes to file permissions, attributes, ownership and group. The parameters in

    this section track changes for system calls that affect file permissions and attributes.

    The following commands and system calls effect the permissions, ownership and

    various attributes of files.

    •

    chmod: changes the mode of the file

    •

    fchmod: changes the mode of the file referred to by the open file descriptor

    •

    fchmodat: change permissions of a file

    •

    fchmodat2: change permissions of a file relative to a directory file descriptor

    In all cases, an audit record will only be written for non-system user ids and will ignore

    Daemon events. All audit records will be tagged with the identifier "perm_mod."

    理由

    Monitoring for changes in file attributes could alert a system administrator to activity that

    could indicate intruder activity or policy violation.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206,

    SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000064-GPOS-00033, SRG-OS-000466-

    GPOS-00210

    审计

    Note:

    •

    Output showing all audited syscalls, e.g. (-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,fchmodat2,setxattr,ls

    etxattr,fsetxattr,removexattr,lremovexattr,fremovexattr -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod) is also acceptable. These

    have been separated by function on the displayed output for clarity.

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exits in the running configuration

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'chmod'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,fchmodat2 -F auid>=1000 -

    F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'chmod' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_mod.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,fchmodat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod

    修复措施

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor discretionary access control

    permission modification events.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,fchmodat2 -F auid>=1000 -

    F auid!=unset -k perm_mod

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_mod.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030490 | RULE ID: SV-230456r810462 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030200 | RULE ID: SV-230413r810463 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654015 | RULE ID: SV-258177r1045316 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654025 | RULE ID: SV-258179r1045322 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048640 | RULE ID: SV-269480r1050363 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: ALMA-09-051390 | RULE ID: SV-269505r1050388 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654160 | RULE ID: SV-260634r958446 | CAT II

    10. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654180 | RULE ID: SV-260638r958446 | CAT II

    11. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900140 | RULE ID: SV-270785r1068373 | CAT II

    12. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900130 | RULE ID: SV-270784r1068371 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.19 确保 discretionary access control permission

    原文: Ensure discretionary access control permission

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor changes to file permissions, attributes, ownership and group. The parameters in

    this section track changes for system calls that affect file permissions and attributes.

    The following commands and system calls effect the permissions, ownership and

    various attributes of files.

    •

    chown: changes the user and/or group ownership of each given file

    •

    fchown: changes the ownership of the file referred to by the open file descriptor

    •

    fchownat: change ownership of a file relative to a directory file descriptor

    •

    lchown: change the owner and group of a file

    In all cases, an audit record will only be written for non-system user ids and will ignore

    Daemon events. All audit records will be tagged with the identifier "perm_mod."

    理由

    Monitoring for changes in file attributes could alert a system administrator to activity that

    could indicate intruder activity or policy violation.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206,

    SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000064-GPOS-00033, SRG-OS-000466-

    GPOS-00210

    审计

    Note:

    •

    Output showing all audited syscalls, e.g. (-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,setxattr,lsetxattr,fs

    etxattr,removexattr,lremovexattr,fremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod) is also acceptable. These have been

    separated by function on the displayed output for clarity.

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exits in the running configuration

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'chown'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chown,fchown,lchown,fchownat -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'chown' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_mod.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    chown,fchown,lchown,fchownat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod

    修复措施

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor discretionary access control

    permission modification events.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chown,fchown,lchown,fchownat -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset -k perm_mod

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_mod.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030490 | RULE ID: SV-230456r810462 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030200 | RULE ID: SV-230413r810463 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654015 | RULE ID: SV-258177r1045316 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654025 | RULE ID: SV-258179r1045322 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048640 | RULE ID: SV-269480r1050363 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: ALMA-09-051390 | RULE ID: SV-269505r1050388 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654160 | RULE ID: SV-260634r958446 | CAT II

    10. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654180 | RULE ID: SV-260638r958446 | CAT II

    11. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900140 | RULE ID: SV-270785r1068373 | CAT II

    12. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900130 | RULE ID: SV-270784r1068371 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.20 确保 discretionary access control permission

    原文: Ensure discretionary access control permission

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor changes to file permissions, attributes, ownership and group. The parameters in

    this section track changes for system calls that affect file permissions and attributes.

    The following commands and system calls effect the permissions, ownership and

    various attributes of files.

    •

    setxattr

    •

    lsetxattr

    •

    fsetxattr

    •

    removexattr

    •

    lremovexattr

    •

    fremovexattr

    In all cases, an audit record will only be written for non-system user ids and will ignore

    Daemon events. All audit records will be tagged with the identifier "perm_mod."

    理由

    Monitoring for changes in file attributes could alert a system administrator to activity that

    could indicate intruder activity or policy violation.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206,

    SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000064-GPOS-00033, SRG-OS-000466-

    GPOS-00210

    审计

    Note:

    •

    Output showing all audited syscalls, e.g. (-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,chmod,fchmod,fchmodat,setxattr,lsetxattr,fs

    etxattr,removexattr,lremovexattr,fremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod) is also acceptable. These have been

    separated by function on the displayed output for clarity.

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exits in the running configuration

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'xattr'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    setxattr,lsetxattr,fsetxattr,removexattr,lremovexattr,fremovexattr -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'xattr' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_mod.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    setxattr,lsetxattr,fsetxattr,removexattr,lremovexattr,fremovexattr -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod

    修复措施

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor discretionary access control

    permission modification events.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    setxattr,lsetxattr,fsetxattr,removexattr,lremovexattr,fremovexattr -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_mod.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030490 | RULE ID: SV-230456r810462 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030200 | RULE ID: SV-230413r810463 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654015 | RULE ID: SV-258177r1045316 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654025 | RULE ID: SV-258179r1045322 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048640 | RULE ID: SV-269480r1050363 | CAT II

    8. STIG ID: ALMA-09-051390 | RULE ID: SV-269505r1050388 | CAT II

    9. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654160 | RULE ID: SV-260634r958446 | CAT II

    10. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654180 | RULE ID: SV-260638r958446 | CAT II

    11. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900140 | RULE ID: SV-270785r1068373 | CAT II

    12. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900130 | RULE ID: SV-270784r1068371 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.21 确保 successful file system mounts are collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure successful file system mounts are collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor the use of the mount system call. The mount (and umount ) system call controls

    the mounting and unmounting of file systems. The parameters below configure the

    system to create an audit record when the mount system call is used by a non-

    privileged user

    理由

    It is highly unusual for a non privileged user to mount file systems to the system. While

    tracking mount commands gives the system administrator evidence that external media

    may have been mounted (based on a review of the source of the mount and confirming

    it's an external media type), it does not conclusively indicate that data was exported to

    the media. System administrators who wish to determine if data were exported, would

    also have to track successful open, creat and truncate system calls requiring write

    access to a file under the mount point of the external media file system. This could give

    a fair indication that a write occurred. The only way to truly prove it, would be to track

    successful writes to the external media. Tracking write system calls could quickly fill up

    the audit log and is not recommended. Recommendations on configuration options to

    track data export to media is beyond the scope of this document.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exits in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '\-S mount'

    Verify the output matches:

    -a always,exit -S mount -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '\-S mount' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-mounts.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S mount -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k mounts

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1.Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor successful file system mounts.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S mount -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k mounts

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-mounts.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030302 | RULE ID: SV-230425r627750 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.3 Enable Detailed Logging

    Enable system logging to include detailed information such as an event source,

    date, user, timestamp, source addresses, destination addresses, and other useful

    elements.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.22 确保 session initiation information is collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure session initiation information is collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor session initiation events. The parameters in this section track changes to the

    files associated with session events.

    •

    /var/run/utmp - tracks all currently logged in users.

    •

    /var/log/wtmp - file tracks logins, logouts, shutdown, and reboot events.

    •

    /var/log/btmp - keeps track of failed login attempts and can be read by

    entering the command /usr/bin/last -f /var/log/btmp.

    All audit records will be tagged with the identifier "session."

    理由

    Monitoring these files for changes could alert a system administrator to logins occurring

    at unusual hours, which could indicate intruder activity (i.e. a user logging in at a time

    when they do not normally log in).

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps --

    '(\/var\/run\/utmp|\/var\/log\/wtmp|\/var\/log\/btmp)'

    Verify the output matches:

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/run/utmp -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/log/wtmp -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/log/btmp -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '(\/var\/run\/utmp|\/var\/log\/wtmp|\/var\/log\/btmp)'

    /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Verify the output matches:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-session.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/var/run/utmp -F perm=wa -k session 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-session.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F 
path=/var/log/wtmp -F perm=wa -k session 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-session.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F 
path=/var/log/btmp -F perm=wa -k session

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor session initiation information.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/run/utmp -F perm=wa -k session

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/log/wtmp -F perm=wa -k session

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/log/btmp -F perm=wa -k session

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-session.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, AU-3

    2. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900600 | RULE ID: SV-270811r1066922 | CAT II

    3. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654205 | RULE ID: SV-260643r991581 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900590 | RULE ID: SV-270810r1066919 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654200 | RULE ID: SV-260642r991581 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900610 | RULE ID: SV-270812r1066925 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654195 | RULE ID: SV-260641r991581 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.9 Log and Alert on Unsuccessful Administrative Account

    Login

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert on unsuccessful logins to an

    administrative account.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    16.13 Alert on Account Login Behavior Deviation

    Alert when users deviate from normal login behavior, such as time-of-day,

    workstation location and duration.

    ●

    6.2.3.23 确保 login and logout events are collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure login and logout events are collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor login and logout events. The parameters below track changes to files

    associated with login/logout events.

    •

    /var/log/lastlog - maintain records of the last time a user successfully logged

    in.

    •

    /var/run/faillock - directory maintains records of login failures via the

    pam_faillock module.

    理由

    Monitoring login/logout events could provide a system administrator with information

    associated with brute force attacks against user logins.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000473-

    GPOS-00218

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '(lastlog|faillock)'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/log/lastlog -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F path=/var/run/faillock -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '(lastlog|faillock)' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-login.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/var/log/lastlog -F perm=wa -k logins 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-login.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=bs4 -S all -F 
path=/var/run/faillock -F perm=wa -k logins

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor login and logout events.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/log/lastlog -F perm=wa -k

    logins

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/var/run/faillock -F perm=wa -k

    logins

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-login.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030600 | RULE ID: SV-230467r627750 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654255 | RULE ID: SV-258225r1014990 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-047540 | RULE ID: SV-269470r1050353 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900260 | RULE ID: SV-270797r1066880 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654215 | RULE ID: SV-260645r958446 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    4.9 Log and Alert on Unsuccessful Administrative Account

    Login

    Configure systems to issue a log entry and alert on unsuccessful logins to an

    administrative account.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    16.11 Lock Workstation Sessions After Inactivity

    Automatically lock workstation sessions after a standard period of inactivity.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    v7

    16.13 Alert on Account Login Behavior Deviation

    Alert when users deviate from normal login behavior, such as time-of-day,

    workstation location and duration.

    ●

    6.2.3.24 确保 unlink file deletion events by users are collected 自动化

    原文: Ensure unlink file deletion events by users are collected (Automated)

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor the use of system calls associated with the deletion or renaming of files and file

    attributes. This configuration statement sets up monitoring for:

    •

    unlink - remove a file

    •

    unlinkat - remove a file attribute

    system calls and tags them with the identifier "delete".

    理由

    Monitoring these calls from non-privileged users could provide a system administrator

    with evidence that inappropriate removal of files and file attributes associated with

    protected files is occurring. While this audit option will look at all events, system

    administrators will want to look for specific privileged files that are being deleted or

    altered.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206,

    SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215

    审计

    Note:

    •

    Output showing all audited syscalls, e.g. (-a always,exit -S

    unlink,unlinkat,rename,renameat,renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset) is also acceptable. These have been separated by function on the

    displayed output for clarity.

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'unlink'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -S unlink,unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'unlink' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-delete.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    unlink,unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k delete

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor file deletion events by users.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlink,unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k

    delete

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-delete.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030361 | RULE ID: SV-230439r810465 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654065 | RULE ID: SV-258187r1045346 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048970 | RULE ID: SV-269483r1050366 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654185 | RULE ID: SV-260639r991577 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900540 | RULE ID: SV-270809r1068388 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.25 确保 rename file deletion events by users are

    原文: Ensure rename file deletion events by users are

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor the use of system calls associated with the deletion or renaming of files and file

    attributes. This configuration statement sets up monitoring for:

    •

    rename: rename a file

    •

    renameat: rename a file attribute

    •

    renameat2: rename a file relative to directory file descriptors

    system calls and tags them with the identifier "delete".

    理由

    Monitoring these calls from non-privileged users could provide a system administrator

    with evidence that inappropriate removal of files and file attributes associated with

    protected files is occurring. While this audit option will look at all events, system

    administrators will want to look for specific privileged files that are being deleted or

    altered.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172, SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206,

    SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215

    审计

    Note:

    •

    Output showing all audited syscalls, e.g. (-a always,exit -S

    unlink,unlinkat,rename,renameat,renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset) is also acceptable. These have been separated by function on the

    displayed output for clarity.

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'rename'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -S rename,renameat,renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'rename' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-delete.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    rename,renameat,renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k delete

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor file deletion events by users.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S rename,renameat,renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset -k delete

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-delete.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030361 | RULE ID: SV-230439r810465 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654065 | RULE ID: SV-258187r1045346 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048970 | RULE ID: SV-269483r1050366 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654185 | RULE ID: SV-260639r991577 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900540 | RULE ID: SV-270809r1068388 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.26 确保 events that modify the system's Mandatory

    原文: Ensure events that modify the system's Mandatory

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor AppArmor, an implementation of mandatory access controls. The parameters

    below monitor any write access (potential additional, deletion or modification of files in

    the directory) or attribute changes to the /etc/apparmor/ and /etc/apparmor.d/

    directories.

    Note: If a different Mandatory Access Control method is used, changes to the

    corresponding directories should be audited.

    理由

    Changes to files in the /etc/apparmor/ and /etc/apparmor.d/ directories could

    indicate that an unauthorized user is attempting to modify access controls and change

    security contexts, leading to a compromise of the system.

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'apparmor'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/etc/apparmor -F perm=wa

    -a always,exit -F dir=/etc/apparmor.d -F perm=wa

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- 'apparmor' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-MAC-policy.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/etc/apparmor -F perm=wa -k MAC-policy 
/etc/audit/rules.d/50-MAC-policy.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F 
dir=/etc/apparmor.d -F perm=wa -k MAC-policy

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor events that modify the system's

    Mandatory Access Controls.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/etc/apparmor -F perm=wa -k MAC-

    policy

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F dir=/etc/apparmor.d -F perm=wa -k MAC-

    policy

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-MAC-policy.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-3, CM-6

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    5.5 Implement Automated Configuration Monitoring

    Systems

    Utilize a Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) compliant configuration

    monitoring system to verify all security configuration elements, catalog approved

    exceptions, and alert when unauthorized changes occur.

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.27 确保 successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    原文: Ensure successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    The operating system must generate audit records for successful/unsuccessful uses of

    the chcon command.

    理由

    The chcon command is used to change file security context. Without generating audit

    records that are specific to the security and needs of the organization, it would be

    difficult to establish, correlate, and investigate the events relating to an incident or

    identify those responsible for one.

    Audit records can be generated from various components within the information system

    (e.g., module or policy filter).

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000468-GPOS-00212, SRG-OS-000471-

    GPOS-00215

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exits in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/chcon'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/chcon -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/chcon' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_chng.rules:-a always,exit -S all -F

    path=/usr/bin/chcon -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_chng

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor successful and unsuccessful

    attempts to use the chcon command.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/usr/bin/chcon -F perm=x -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_chng

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_chng.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030260 | RULE ID: SV-230419r627750 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654045 | RULE ID: SV-258183r1045334 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048420 | RULE ID: SV-269478r1050361 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654025 | RULE ID: SV-260607r958446 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900210 | RULE ID: SV-270792r1066865 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.2 Collect Audit Logs

    Collect audit logs. Ensure that logging, per the enterprise’s audit log

    management process, has been enabled across enterprise assets.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.28 确保 successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    原文: Ensure successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    The operating system must generate audit records for successful/unsuccessful uses of

    the setfacl command

    理由

    This utility sets Access Control Lists (ACLs) of files and directories. Without generating

    audit records that are specific to the security and mission needs of the organization, it

    would be difficult to establish, correlate, and investigate the events relating to an

    incident or identify those responsible for one.

    Audit records can be generated from various components within the information system

    (e.g., module or policy filter).

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/setfacl'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/setfacl -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/setfacl' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_chng.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/usr/bin/setfacl -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_chng

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor successful and unsuccessful

    attempts to use the setfacl command.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/usr/bin/setfacl -F perm=x -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_chng

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_chng.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-2, AU-12, SI-5

    2. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030330 | RULE ID: SV-230435r627750 | CAT II

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654040 | RULE ID: SV-258182r1045331 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: ALMA-09-050180 | RULE ID: SV-269494r1050377 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654085 | RULE ID: SV-260619r958446 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900230 | RULE ID: SV-270794r1066871 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.2 Collect Audit Logs

    Collect audit logs. Ensure that logging, per the enterprise’s audit log

    management process, has been enabled across enterprise assets.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.29 确保 successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    原文: Ensure successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    The operating system must generate audit records for successful/unsuccessful uses of

    the chacl command.

    chacl is an IRIX-compatibility command, and is maintained for those users who are

    familiar with its use from either XFS or IRIX.

    理由

    chacl changes the ACL(s) for a file or directory. Without generating audit records that

    are specific to the security and mission needs of the organization, it would be difficult to

    establish, correlate, and investigate the events relating to an incident or identify those

    responsible for one.

    Audit records can be generated from various components within the information system

    (e.g., module or policy filter).

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000466-

    GPOS-00210

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/chacl'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/chacl -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/chacl' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_chng.rules:-a always,exit -S all -F

    path=/usr/bin/chacl -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_chng

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor successful and unsuccessful

    attempts to use the chacl command.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/usr/bin/chacl -F perm=x -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_chng

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-perm_chng.rules

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-2, AU-12, SI-5

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030570 | RULE ID: SV-230464r627750 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654035 | RULE ID: SV-258181r1045328 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-048200 | RULE ID: SV-269476r1050359 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654015 | RULE ID: SV-260605r958446 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900240 | RULE ID: SV-270795r1066874 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.2 Collect Audit Logs

    Collect audit logs. Ensure that logging, per the enterprise’s audit log

    management process, has been enabled across enterprise assets.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.30 确保 successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    原文: Ensure successful and unsuccessful attempts to use the

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    The operating system must generate audit records for successful/unsuccessful uses of

    the usermod command.

    理由

    The usermod command modifies the system account files to reflect the changes that are

    specified on the command line. Without generating audit records that are specific to the

    security and needs of the organization, it would be difficult to establish, correlate, and

    investigate the events relating to an incident or identify those responsible for one.

    Audit records can be generated from various components within the information system

    (e.g., module or policy filter).

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000466-

    GPOS-00210

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/sbin\/usermod'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/usr/sbin/usermod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F

    auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/sbin\/usermod' 
/etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-usermod.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F

    path=/usr/sbin/usermod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k usermod

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor successful and unsuccessful

    attempts to use the usermod command.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/usr/sbin/usermod -F perm=x -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k usermod

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-usermod.rules unset

    Note: The auid>= value should be set to the same value as UID_MIN value in

    /etc/login.defs.

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    3. STIG ID: RHEL-08-030560 | RULE ID: SV-230463r627750 | CAT II

    4. STIG ID: RHEL-09-654175 | RULE ID: SV-258209r1045412 | CAT II

    5. STIG ID: ALMA-09-051280 | RULE ID: SV-269504r1050387 | CAT II

    6. STIG ID: UBTU-22-654125 | RULE ID: SV-260627r958446 | CAT II

    7. STIG ID: UBTU-24-900310 | RULE ID: SV-270802r1066895 | CAT II

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.2 Collect Audit Logs

    Collect audit logs. Ensure that logging, per the enterprise’s audit log

    management process, has been enabled across enterprise assets.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.31 确保 kernel module loading unloading and

    原文: Ensure kernel module loading unloading and

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    Monitor the loading and unloading of kernel modules. All the loading / listing /

    dependency checking of modules is done by kmod via symbolic links.

    理由

    Monitoring the use of all the various ways to manipulate kernel modules could provide

    system administrators with evidence that an unauthorized change was made to a kernel

    module, possibly compromising the security of the system.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000471-

    GPOS-00216, SRG-OS-000477-GPOS-00222

    审计

    Note: The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/kmod'

    Verify the output includes:

    -a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/kmod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:

    # grep -Ps -- '^\h*[^#\n\r]+\h*\/usr\/bin\/kmod' /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules

    Example output:

    /etc/audit/rules.d/50-kernel_modules.rules:-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all

    -F path=/usr/bin/kmod -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k

    kernel_modules

    Symlink audit

    Run the following script to audit if the symlinks kmod accepts are indeed pointing at it:

    #!/usr/bin/env bash 
 
{ 
   a_files=("/usr/sbin/lsmod" "/usr/sbin/rmmod" "/usr/sbin/insmod"

    "/usr/sbin/modinfo" "/usr/sbin/modprobe" "/usr/sbin/depmod")

    for l_file in "${a_files[@]}"; do 
      if [ "$(readlink -f "$l_file")" = "$(readlink -f /bin/kmod)" ]; then 
         printf "OK: \"$l_file\"\n" 
      else 
         printf "Issue with symlink for file: \"$l_file\"\n" 
      fi 
   done 
}

    Verify the output states OK. If there is a symlink pointing to a different location it should

    be investigated

    修复措施

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    For best performance, the arch field should be supplied in the rule. The

    individual permissions will cause the selection of specific system calls that use

    that kind of access. Not supplying the arch will cause the selection of all system

    calls which will affect performance as all system calls will be evaluated.

    1. Edit or create a file in the /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory, ending in .rules

    extension, with the relevant rules to monitor kernel module modification.

    Example:

    # printf "

    -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S all -F path=/usr/bin/kmod -F perm=x -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k kernel_modules

    " >> /etc/audit/rules.d/50-kernel_modules.rules

    2. Merge and load the rules into active configuration:

    # augenrules --load

    3. Check if reboot is required:

    # if [[ $(auditctl -s | grep "enabled") =~ "2" ]]; then printf "Reboot

    required to load rules\n"; fi

    参考资料

    1. NIST SP 800-53 :: AU-12 a

    2. NIST SP 800-53A :: AU-12.1 (ii)

    附加信息

    Potential reboot required

    If the auditing configuration is locked (-e 2), then augenrules will not warn in any way

    that rules could not be loaded into the running configuration. A system reboot will be

    required to load the rules into the running configuration.

    System call structure

    For performance (man 7 audit.rules) reasons it is preferable to have all the system

    calls on one line. However, your configuration may have them on one line each or some

    other combination. This is important to understand for both the auditing and remediation

    sections as the examples given are optimized for performance as per the man page.

    CIS 控制项

    Controls

    Version

    Control

    IG 1 IG 2 IG 3

    v8

    8.5 Collect Detailed Audit Logs

    Configure detailed audit logging for enterprise assets containing sensitive data.

    Include event source, date, username, timestamp, source addresses, destination

    addresses, and other useful elements that could assist in a forensic investigation.

    ●

    ●

    v7

    6.2 Activate audit logging

    Ensure that local logging has been enabled on all systems and networking

    devices.

    ●

    ●

    ●

    6.2.3.32 确保 kernel "init_module" and "finit_module" loading

    原文: Ensure kernel "init_module" and "finit_module" loading

    适用配置文件

  • Level 2 - Server
  • Level 2 - Workstation

    • 描述

    The following system calls control loading and unloading of modules:

    •

    init_module - load a module

    •

    finit_module - load a module (used when the overhead of using

    cryptographically signed modules to determine the authenticity of a module can

    be avoided)

    Any execution of the loading and unloading module programs and system calls will

    trigger an audit record with an identifier of modules.

    理由

    Monitoring the use of all the various ways to manipulate kernel modules could provide

    system administrators with evidence that an unauthorized change was made to a kernel

    module, possibly compromising the security of the system.

    Satisfies: SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000037-GPOS-00015, SRG-OS-

    000042-GPOS-00020, SRG-OS-000062-GPOS-00031, SRG-OS-000392-GPOS-00172,

    SRG-OS-000462-GPOS-00206, SRG-OS-000471-GPOS-00215, SRG-OS-000471-

    GPOS-00216, SRG-OS-000477-GPOS-00222

    审计

    Note:

    •

    The -w option is deprecated and the rule should be updated in accordance with

    the Remediation Procedure.

    •

    Output showing all the audited syscalls, e.g. (-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S

    init_module,finit_module,delete_module,create_module,query_module -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k kernel_modules) is also acceptable. These have

    been separated by function on the displayed output for clarity.

    1. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in the running configuration:

    # auditctl -l | grep -Ps -- 'init_module'

    Verify the output includes init_module and finite_module:

    -a always,exit -S

    init_module,finit_module,delete_module,create_module,query_module -F

    auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset

    2. Run the following command to verify a rule exists in a rules file:
